topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The stack of crumpled papers sat on the kitchen counter like a monument to my failure. Each sheet, a testament to hours of effort that had amounted to nothing, mocked me. I stared at the mountain, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead amplifying the hollow ache in my chest. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy weight pressing them into my neck. "Just give up," I mumbled, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. It was the culmination of weeks of dread—the realization that every single assignment, every test, every essay, had been meticulously saved by my mother. A slow, cold dread began to pool in my stomach, tightening with every glance at the pristine pile. I picked up a worksheet, the ink blurring under my thumb, and felt a profound sense of defeat wash over me.

[story 2]

I shuffled through the hallway, my backpack feeling impossibly heavy, the straps digging into my already aching shoulders. The silence of the house was oppressive, broken only by the frantic drumming in my own ears. I finally found her in the study, a place I rarely ventured into. There, tucked behind a stack of old tax documents, was it: the binder. Every single assignment, from the rudimentary math problems to the sprawling history reports, neatly cataloged and filed. My breath hitched. I walked toward it slowly, my steps dragging. My hands trembled as I reached out, touching the crisp paper. A wave of profound exhaustion washed over me, a sudden, complete draining of energy. I leaned against the doorframe, just breathing, unable to move or speak.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted weakly through the window, illuminating the dust motes dancing above the desk. I was supposed to be studying for the upcoming exam, but all I could manage was staring blankly at the wall. A strange compulsion drew me toward my mother’s office. When I saw the collection—every single piece of schoolwork, preserved like artifacts—a sudden, sharp wave of despair hit me. My jaw tightened involuntarily. I walked over, my feet moving with a sluggishness that spoke volumes about my inner state. I didn't touch anything; I just stood there, absorbing the sheer volume of unseen effort. The quiet acknowledgment of what I had missed, what I hadn't achieved, settled over me like a suffocating blanket.
